title: A note on digital twins when reliability turned out to be the whole game
summary: A reflection on digital twins and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: digital twins when reliability turned out to be the whole game
body: My notes on digital twins began with a small irritation in May 2024. Once everyone began treating the story as self-evident, I started looking for what had been flattened. When a formulation travels instantly, I assume some awkward but important detail got left behind. In this case the omitted piece was the long argument between autonomy and auditability. That phrasing may sound abstract, but it cashes out in very ordinary questions about maintenance, blame, trust, and who gets rewarded for calling all of that progress.

Digital twins appealed to me because it sat right on the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. It is easier to talk about the engineering because engineering already knows how to present itself. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Tools show up carrying assumptions about who will use them well and what kind of world should make that use feel natural. The design tends to confess those assumptions to anyone willing to look closely. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also a question of what sorts of habits and dependencies the system makes feel ordinary.

I kept noticing the moral distance between a prototype and the public environment it eventually enters. The prototype has one advantage, nobody mistakes it for settled reality. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. A tool behaves very differently when it is tried first on people who opted in for the strange version. It is much easier to defend the logic in a demo than in the places where people actually have to live with it. The long argument between autonomy and auditability becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my interest in the topic but my tolerance for abstraction. After writing “On memory systems while everyone tried to make software feel responsible,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. Any claim about the future that collapses on contact with ordinary life does not earn much trust from me. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. Still, it matters to notice when a useful tool crosses the line into quiet obligation. Digital twins seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often the shift is relational before it is technological, and it becomes normal before it becomes examined.

A final note to myself from May 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. What holds my attention now is not the prediction itself but the burden a tool shifts onto the people nearest it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

I keep coming back to fragile supply chains
